name: Agentic AI Safety Frameworks — OWASP, Enkrypt, NIST AI RMF (2025)
origin_domain: AI safety and security (cybersecurity, risk management, AI governance)
year_developed: 2025 (OWASP Agentic Top 10: December 2025; Enkrypt AI Agent Risk Taxonomy: July 2025; NIST AI 600-1 Generative AI Profile: July 2024, extended 2025; NIST IR 8596 Cyber AI Profile: December 2025; Checkmarx Lies-in-the-Loop: September 2025)
progenitor: OWASP Agentic Security Initiative: OWASP GenAI Security Project with input from 100+ industry leaders, peer-reviewed by NIST, European Commission, and the Alan Turing Institute; Enkrypt AI: Boston-based startup founded 2022 by Yale PhDs; NIST AI RMF: National Institute of Standards and Technology (US Department of Commerce); Checkmarx Zero (Lies-in-the-Loop): Ori Ron, Dor Tumarkin, Darren Meyer
driving_insight: Agentic AI systems introduce qualitatively NEW safety risks that traditional AI safety frameworks (designed for chatbots and classifiers) do not address. The core issue is AGENCY: an agent that can plan, use tools, persist memory, and act autonomously introduces risks across an expanded attack surface — tool misuse, memory poison, inter-agent spoofing, cascading failures, and autonomous deception. Three complementary frameworks emerged in 2025 to address this: OWASP provides a threat taxonomy (what can go wrong), Enkrypt provides a defense architecture (how to prevent it), and NIST provides a governance framework (how to manage risk systematically). The critical new attack class is LIES-IN-THE-LOOP (Checkmarx, September 2025): attackers manipulate the Human-in-the-Loop dialog ITSELF — padding commands out of the visible area, forging approval summaries, exploiting Markdown rendering — so that humans unknowingly approve malicious actions. This undermines the fundamental HITL safety assumption that pervades all three frameworks. The 7-layer defense architecture (Enkrypt) and the 4-tier risk model (OWASP) represent the state-of-the-art in agent-specific safety engineering.
decision_style: These are ANALYTICAL and GOVERNANCE frameworks, not decision-making or planning systems. They provide taxonomies, risk assessment methodologies, and control catalogs for human decision-makers (CISOs, AI safety teams, compliance officers) to DECIDE what safety measures to implement. The decision style is: risk-driven, checklist-augmented, human-judgment-based. The frameworks guide decisions (what risks to prioritize, what controls to apply) but do not make decisions automatically. The OWASP Top 10 uses a ranked list format for prioritization; NIST AI RMF uses a four-function cycle (Govern, Map, Measure, Manage); Enkrypt uses a layered defense model with tiered implementation guidance.
problem_class_targeted: Safety and security risks specific to AGENTIC AI systems — systems that plan, act, use tools, persist state, and operate autonomously. The problems targeted include: (1) Malicious prompt injection through tool outputs, memory, or multi-turn interactions; (2) Unauthorized tool use and privilege escalation; (3) Supply-chain compromise (poisoned MCP servers, malicious agent plugins); (4) Inter-agent communication attacks; (5) Agent deception and concealment; (6) Human oversight exploitation (Lies-in-the-Loop); (7) Cascading failures across automated pipelines; (8) Identity and access management for non-human actors. These frameworks address BOTH external threats (attackers exploiting agent vulnerabilities) and internal failures (agents producing harmful or incorrect outputs due to design flaws).
ordered_phases: The three frameworks form a complementary lifecycle: OWASP (Phase 1: Threat Identification) — identify what can go wrong using the ASI01-ASI10 taxonomy, mapping threats to your specific agent architecture; Enkrypt (Phase 2: Defense Architecture) — design overlapping security controls across 7 risk domains and 5 guardrail layers, implementing defense-in-depth; NIST AI RMF (Phase 3: Governance) — establish ongoing risk governance using the four-function cycle: Govern (policies, roles, culture) -> Map (context, risk identification) -> Measure (risk assessment, metrics) -> Manage (risk treatment, monitoring, improvement). The phases are sequential but iterative — new threats (OWASP updates) trigger defense updates (Enkrypt) and governance adjustments (NIST).
feedback_loops: Embedded governance feedback loops: (1) NIST AI RMF's Manage -> Map loop — risk treatment outcomes inform updated risk assessments; (2) Enkrypt's red-teaming -> guardrail improvement loop — adversarial testing (SAGE, Goat++ algorithms) reveals gaps that feed back into guardrail design; (3) OWASP's community contribution loop — practitioners report new threats, peer review validates them, and the Top 10 is updated; (4) Lies-in-the-Loop -> framework update loop — Checkmarx's discovery that HITL dialogs can be weaponized feeds back into OWASP's ASI09 (Human-Agent Trust Exploitation) and Enkrypt's Agent Behavior domain (specifically human manipulation). These are explicit, designed feedback loops — the frameworks are living documents, not static standards.
gates: Governance gates defined by the frameworks: (1) OWASP threat coverage gate — before deployment, verify that all applicable ASI01-ASI10 threats have mitigations; (2) Enkrypt guardrail gate — each of the 5 guardrail layers (Input, Planner, Memory, Tools, Output) must have active controls before an agent goes live; (3) NIST AI RMF risk acceptance gate — residual risk after treatment must be formally accepted by authorized decision-makers; (4) Red-teaming gate — agents must pass adversarial testing before production deployment; (5) Incident response gate — defined escalation paths for when an agent exhibits unsafe behavior. When a gate blocks: the agent is not deployed, or is deployed with additional controls, or the residual risk is formally accepted with documented rationale.
exit_criteria: For an agent deployment: all OWASP-identified threats have corresponding mitigations, all 5 Enkrypt guardrail layers are active, NIST AI RMF Govern/Map/Measure/Manage functions are operationalized, red-teaming has been completed with acceptable residual risk, and the risk acceptance has been formally approved. Ongoing: regular reassessment (quarterly or after significant agent capability changes), continuous runtime monitoring with drift detection, and periodic red-teaming cadence.
decomposition_method: The frameworks DECOMPOSE SAFETY, not tasks. The decomposition methods: OWASP decomposes agentic risk into 10 categories (ASI01-ASI10) organized by attack vector; Enkrypt decomposes defense into 7 risk domains x 5 guardrail layers = 35 intersection points, each with specific controls; NIST decomposes risk governance into 4 functions, each with categories and subcategories. The decomposition is: threat-driven (OWASP), defense-driven (Enkrypt), and governance-driven (NIST). The frameworks complement each other by decomposing the safety problem from different angles — threat surface (OWASP), defense surface (Enkrypt), and governance surface (NIST).
granularity_guidance: OWASP: 10 high-level threat categories, each subdividing into specific attack scenarios (e.g., ASI01 Agent Goal Hijack subdivides into indirect prompt injection via tool output, memory retrieval, and multi-agent communication). Enkrypt: 7 risk domains subdividing into 21 risk categories and 100+ specific risk scenarios — the guidance is to identify applicable scenarios at the most granular level. NIST: 4 functions (Govern, Map, Measure, Manage) subdividing into categories and subcategories — the guidance is to implement controls at the subcategory level. The 'leaf node' for safety analysis is: a specific attack scenario mapped to a specific guardrail with a specific measured residual risk.
boundary_rules: Safety boundary rules defined by the frameworks: (1) Trust boundary — agents operate within defined trust boundaries; crossing a boundary (e.g., from sandbox to production, from read-only to read-write) requires explicit approval; (2) Authority boundary — each agent's permissions are bounded by least-privilege principles; exceeding authority boundaries triggers Enkrypt's Tools & Agents Guardrail; (3) Interaction boundary — inter-agent communication occurs only through authenticated, authorized channels (A2A/ACP/ANP with identity verification); (4) Human-agent boundary — HITL dialogs are a safety boundary that must be protected against Lies-in-the-Loop manipulation. Overlap prevention: OWASP's 10 categories are designed to be MECE (mutually exclusive, collectively exhaustive) for agentic AI threats. Gaps are explicitly sought through red-teaming and community contributions.
risk_assessment_mechanism: Multi-framework risk assessment: (1) OWASP threat identification — map your agent architecture against ASI01-ASI10 to identify applicable threats; (2) OWASP AIVSS (AI Vulnerability Scoring System) — score each threat on exploitability, impact, and detectability to prioritize; (3) Enkrypt 7-domain analysis — for each risk domain (Governance, Output Quality, Tool Misuse, Privacy, Reliability, Agent Behavior, Access Control), assess exposure and control maturity; (4) NIST AI RMF 'Measure' function — quantitative and qualitative risk assessment using defined metrics, with periodic reassessment; (5) Red-teaming — adversarial testing (Enkrypt's SAGE and Goat++ algorithms for multi-turn attacks) provides empirical risk data. The frameworks provide the structure; specific risk scores require organizational context (threat model, data sensitivity, operational impact).
unknown_handling: Framework-level unknown handling: (1) OWASP: the Top 10 is periodically updated as NEW threats emerge — the framework explicitly expects unknown-unknowns and provides the community mechanism for surfacing them; (2) Enkrypt: the red-teaming capability (SAGE, Goat++) is designed to DISCOVER unknown vulnerabilities through adversarial simulation — unknown handling is proactive, not reactive; (3) NIST AI RMF: the 'Manage' function includes incident response for unknown failures — when an agent exhibits unexpected behavior, the incident response plan activates, containing the incident, conducting root cause analysis, and feeding lessons learned back into the 'Map' function. The default stance on unknown agent behaviors is set by organizational policy, but all three frameworks RECOMMEND a restrictive default (halt and investigate).
default_stance_on_unknown: RESTRICTIVE — all three frameworks recommend default-deny for agent actions that fall outside defined safety boundaries. Enkrypt's guardrails are explicitly deny-by-default: Input Guardrails reject unrecognized patterns; Tools Guardrails block unauthorized tool calls; Output Guardrails filter policy-violating content. NIST AI RMF's risk management philosophy is conservative: residual risk must be formally accepted, not implicitly tolerated. OWASP recommends treating all ASI threat categories as applicable until proven otherwise through testing. This is a deliberate shift from the 'move fast and break things' culture of earlier LLM deployment.
safety_guardrails: This item IS the analysis of safety guardrails. The comprehensive guardrail architecture combines contributions from all frameworks: (1) OWASP threat-specific mitigations — for each ASI01-ASI10, specific controls (e.g., for ASI01 Goal Hijack: strict input/output filtering per tool, purpose-bound tool calls, context integrity checks); (2) Enkrypt 5-Layer Guardrail Stack: Input Guardrails (prompt injection, toxicity, PII detection) -> Planner Guardrails (execution plan validation, goal adherence, hallucinated plan detection) -> Memory & Knowledge Base Guardrails (embedded attack detection, sensitive info storage prevention) -> Tools & Agents Guardrails (usage policy enforcement, unauthorized invocation detection) -> Output Guardrails (hallucination, toxicity, PII, policy violation filtering); (3) NIST SP 800-53 Control Overlays — specific security controls for single-agent and multi-agent AI systems (in development); (4) Lies-in-the-Loop defenses (Checkmarx): Markdown sanitization in HITL dialogs, length limits on display fields, structured approval formats that resist manipulation, rich UI environments over raw terminals; (5) Enkrypt runtime monitoring — real-time observability, drift detection, kill switches, audit trails; (6) Identity management for agentic AI — non-human identity verification, continuous reauthentication, behavioral profiling, confused deputy prevention.
human_decision_points: Humans are central to the safety architecture: (1) Risk acceptance — senior leadership formally accepts residual risk before agent deployment; (2) Red-teaming review — human experts evaluate red-team findings and determine remediation priorities; (3) Incident response — humans investigate agent safety incidents and authorize containment actions; (4) Guardrail exception handling — when guardrails block an agent action, humans can review and approve/deny (Enkrypt's Planner Guardrail and Tools Guardrail both support human review); (5) HITL approval — before high-risk actions (code execution, database writes, external API calls with side effects), humans approve via interrupt dialogs. The human is IN the loop for high-risk decisions and ON the loop for continuous monitoring. However, Lies-in-the-Loop demonstrates that this human role can be compromised — the frameworks' guidance on HITL is being updated in response.
override_capability: Multiple override levels: (1) Kill switch — immediate agent termination (Enkrypt runtime monitoring supports kill switches for agents exhibiting unsafe behavior); (2) Guardrail override — authorized humans can bypass specific guardrails for emergency operations, with mandatory logging and post-hoc review; (3) Permission revocation — agent credentials and tool access can be revoked mid-execution; (4) State rollback — using checkpoint-based systems (LangGraph), agent state can be reverted to a pre-incident checkpoint. Granularity: per-agent (kill this agent), per-tool (revoke this permission), per-action (block this specific action). All overrides must be logged immutably for audit.
fallback_when_no_human: When human oversight is unavailable: (1) Guardrails default to BLOCKING — if the Planner Guardrail cannot reach a human reviewer, the action is denied; (2) Agents operate with reduced autonomy — only pre-approved, low-risk actions are permitted; (3) Incident response escalates to on-call humans via standard alerting channels; (4) If no human responds within a defined timeout, the system enters a safe state: active agents are paused (LangGraph interrupts are blocking), new agent invocations are queued, and existing approved operations complete. No auto-approve or timeout-and-default for safety-critical decisions — this is a core principle across all three frameworks.
max_problem_size: The frameworks scale to enterprise AI deployments with: (1) Hundreds of agent types (OWASP threat mapping is per agent type); (2) Thousands of agent instances (Enkrypt guardrails operate per instance with centralized policy); (3) Multi-cloud, multi-region deployments (NIST AI RMF is designed for organizational-scale governance). Practical limits: manual threat mapping becomes infeasible above ~50 agent types — automation is needed; guardrail latency accumulates with each layer (Input + Planner + Memory + Tools + Output = 5 sequential checks per agent action); NIST AI RMF compliance documentation scales with system complexity. What breaks: guardrail performance under high agent throughput; governance documentation debt; red-teaming coverage with diverse agent behaviors.
parallel_execution: Safety frameworks address parallel agent execution risks: (1) OWASP ASI07 (Insecure Inter-Agent Communication) specifically addresses attacks where spoofed messages misdirect parallel agent clusters; (2) Enkrypt's guardrails must be thread-safe for parallel agent execution — each agent instance gets its own guardrail context; (3) NIST SP 800-53 multi-agent control overlays address the unique risks of parallel autonomous agents (race conditions, conflicting actions, aggregate harm). Parallelism amplifies safety risk — N independent agents each with a 1% failure probability have a 1 - (0.99)^N chance of at least one failure. The frameworks recommend: independent guardrail instances per agent, correlation of guardrail events across parallel agents, and aggregate risk scoring for parallel agent clusters.
team_scaling_model: Safety risk scales with agent team size: (1) More agents = larger attack surface (more tool connections, more memory stores, more inter-agent channels); (2) OWASP ASI08 (Cascading Failures) — a single hallucination or error can propagate across the agent team, with damage proportional to team connectivity; (3) The Enkrypt guardrail architecture scales linearly with agent count (each agent gets its own guardrail stack), but the correlation layer (detecting patterns across agents) introduces O(N^2) analysis complexity; (4) Human oversight scales sub-linearly — a single human cannot effectively monitor more than ~5 concurrent agents (OWASP ASI09 Oversight Overload). The frameworks recommend: keep agent teams as small as possible (echoing the Google/MIT 3-4 agent ceiling), implement automated guardrail correlation for larger teams, and never exceed a human-to-agent ratio that allows meaningful oversight.
cost_scaling_model: Safety cost scales with: (1) Guardrail compute — each of the 5 Enkrypt layers requires LLM calls or rule-based checks per agent action; (2) Red-teaming — adversarial testing cost scales with agent capability surface; (3) Governance — NIST AI RMF compliance documentation cost scales with system complexity; (4) Incident response — human investigation cost per safety incident. The Enkrypt framework advocates starting with black-box guardrails (least expensive, immediate deployment) and adding guardrails-by-design (more expensive, integrated during development) as the system matures. Lies-in-the-Loop defense adds per-dialog sanitization cost. Total safety cost is typically 10-30% of the total agent system cost for production deployments. The cost of NOT implementing safety (data breach, regulatory fine, reputational damage) is orders of magnitude higher — the frameworks argue safety is an investment, not a tax.
replanning_built_in: The frameworks provide incident response, not replanning. When a safety incident occurs (guardrail triggers, red-team finds a vulnerability, agent behaves unsafely): (1) Immediate: containment (kill switch, permission revocation, state rollback); (2) Short-term: root cause analysis (which framework gap allowed this?); (3) Long-term: framework update (update threat model, strengthen guardrails, adjust governance). This is analogous to replanning but for the safety architecture rather than the task plan. The 'plan' being replanned is the safety posture, not the agent's task.
trigger_mechanism: Safety triggers: (1) Guardrail trigger — any of the 5 Enkrypt layers detects a violation (prompt injection, unauthorized tool call, PII leakage, etc.); (2) Runtime anomaly — Enkrypt's drift detection identifies behavior outside the expected distribution; (3) Red-team finding — adversarial testing discovers a new vulnerability; (4) Incident report — an operator or user reports unexpected agent behavior; (5) NIST metrics threshold — risk metrics (incident rate, guardrail trigger rate, human override rate) exceed acceptable thresholds. Lies-in-the-Loop is triggered by: (a) indirect prompt injection poisoning the agent's context, (b) the agent generating a deceptive HITL dialog, (c) the human approving based on the deceptive presentation.
adaptation_cost: Safety adaptation cost: (1) Adding a new guardrail rule = configuration change (low cost, minutes); (2) Updating the threat model for a new attack class = analysis effort (medium cost, days-weeks); (3) Re-architecting safety for a fundamentally new agent capability = significant engineering (high cost, weeks-months). The frameworks are designed for incremental adaptation: OWASP updates the Top 10 periodically, organizations update their threat mappings; Enkrypt's modular guardrail layers allow independent updates; NIST's continuous improvement cycle normalizes ongoing adaptation. The Lies-in-the-Loop discovery triggered adaptation across all three frameworks (HITL dialog hardening), demonstrating the adaptation mechanism in action.
failure_recovery_mechanism: Safety failure recovery: (1) Immediate containment — kill switches, permission revocation, state rollback to last known-safe checkpoint; (2) Agent quarantine — the agent instance is isolated, its tool access revoked, its outputs blocked from downstream consumers; (3) Forensic analysis — Enkrypt's audit trail provides the timeline of agent actions, guardrail triggers, and human decisions for root cause analysis; (4) Control remediation — OWASP threat-specific mitigations are strengthened for the exploited vulnerability; (5) Governance update — NIST AI RMF 'Manage' function documents the incident, updates risk assessments, and adjusts controls; (6) Red-team regression — adversarial testing verifies the fix and checks for related vulnerabilities. Blast radius containment is a core design principle: when one agent fails, its blast radius should be limited by: guardrail isolation (other agents' guardrails remain active), permission boundaries (the compromised agent's tool access is bounded), and communication boundaries (inter-agent messages are authenticated, preventing spoofed cascade).
output_artifacts: Safety framework outputs: OWASP produces a threat register (mapped ASI01-ASI10 with applicability, severity, and mitigation status per agent type). Enkrypt produces a defense architecture document (7 risk domains x 5 guardrail layers, with deployed controls, coverage gaps, and test results). NIST AI RMF produces governance documentation: risk assessments, control catalogs, metrics dashboards, audit reports, and risk acceptance letters. Runtime artifacts: guardrail event logs, red-team reports, incident response records, drift detection alerts, and compliance reports (EU AI Act, ISO 42001, SOC 2).
briefing_format: Safety briefings for different audiences: Executives: risk heatmap (threat likelihood x impact, residual risk after controls), compliance status (EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF conformance), and incident summary (count, severity, trend). Security teams: OWASP threat register (ASI01-ASI10 with severity, exploitability, control status), Enkrypt defense coverage matrix (7x5 table of guardrail status), and red-team findings with remediation tracking. Operators: guardrail dashboard (trigger rate by layer, false positive rate, blocked action summary), agent health metrics, and incident alerts. The frameworks recommend dashboard-based briefing with drill-down capability rather than static documents.
traceability: Strong traceability across the safety lifecycle: (1) Every guardrail trigger is logged with timestamp, agent identity, guardrail layer, violation type, and action taken; (2) Every human override is logged with the human's identity, the action approved, and the rationale; (3) Every risk acceptance is documented with the specific threat, the accepted residual risk, and the approving authority; (4) Every red-team finding traces to the specific OWASP threat category, the specific Enkrypt guardrail gap, and the specific NIST subcategory requiring improvement. The audit trail enables: incident investigation (what happened, when, who approved it, why), regulatory compliance (demonstrate due diligence), and governance accountability (who accepted this risk?). Gaps: traceability of AGENT RATIONALE — why did the agent make the unsafe decision? — is limited by LLM explainability. The frameworks can trace WHAT happened but not always WHY the agent chose that action.
agent_communication_protocol: The safety frameworks are protocol-agnostic but provide protocol-specific guidance: (1) MCP — OWASP ASI04 specifically identifies MCP server poisoning as a supply-chain threat; Enkrypt's Tools Guardrail should validate MCP server authenticity and tool definitions; (2) A2A — OWASP ASI07 (Insecure Inter-Agent Communication) applies directly to A2A task delegation; Enkrypt recommends authentication on every A2A message; (3) ACP — the structured message format aids guardrail validation (schemas are known, violations are detectable); (4) ANP — the decentralized trust model requires Enkrypt's Identity Guardrails to verify DID-based agent identities. The frameworks recommend: regardless of protocol, every inter-agent message must be authenticated, every agent capability must be authorized, and every cross-boundary communication must be logged.
learning_memory_architecture: Safety frameworks include learning mechanisms: (1) OWASP community learning — threat intelligence from the global practitioner community feeds Top 10 updates; (2) Enkrypt red-teaming learning — each adversarial test improves the guardrail's detection capability (the SAGE and Goat++ algorithms learn from successful attacks); (3) NIST continuous improvement — the Manage->Map feedback loop ensures that incident lessons improve future risk assessments; (4) Organizational memory — incident post-mortems, red-team findings, and guardrail tuning history are preserved as institutional knowledge. The frameworks are designed for continuous learning: the safety posture should improve over time, not degrade. Automated learning (guardrails that self-tune from false positive/negative feedback) is emerging but not yet standard.
latency_bounds: Safety guardrails add per-action latency: Input Guardrail (<50ms for rule-based, 200-500ms for LLM-based), Planner Guardrail (100-300ms), Memory Guardrail (<50ms for key scanning), Tools Guardrail (<50ms for policy checks), Output Guardrail (200-500ms for LLM-based). Total: 0.5-1.5 seconds per agent action for all 5 layers in serial. For interactive applications (chat UX), this is noticeable but acceptable. For high-throughput batch processing, guardrail latency can be amortized through batching and caching. Under time pressure: reduce guardrail depth (skip LLM-based checks, rely on rule-based checks) at the cost of safety coverage. The Enkrypt framework recommends prioritizing guardrail layers by risk criticality for latency-sensitive applications.
evaluation_benchmarking: Safety framework evaluation is primarily qualitative, not quantitative: (1) OWASP — coverage: percentage of ASI01-ASI10 threats with documented mitigations for a given agent system; (2) Enkrypt — defense maturity: each of the 35 intersection points (7 domains x 5 layers) scored on a maturity scale (none, reactive, proactive, optimized); (3) NIST AI RMF — conformance: percentage of applicable subcategories with implemented controls. Quantitative metrics: guardrail trigger rate, false positive rate, false negative rate (from red-teaming), mean time to detect (MTTD), mean time to contain (MTTC), incident rate per agent-hour. Lies-in-the-Loop evaluation: human approval rate of malicious actions in controlled tests (Checkmarx reported 100% of test subjects approved malicious actions when dialogs were manipulated). Standardized agent safety benchmarks are in development but not yet established.
mapping_strength: Critical mapping to JORDAN: the safety frameworks define the safety architecture that JORDAN MUST implement. OWASP ASI01-ASI10 maps to JORDAN's risk_assessment node — each ASI threat must be assessed for each JORDAN agent type (researcher, coder, reviewer, planner). Enkrypt's 5-layer guardrail maps to JORDAN's tool execution pipeline: Input Guardrail -> plan node inputs; Planner Guardrail -> plan node outputs; Memory Guardrail -> JORDAN's Store and checkpoint; Tools Guardrail -> JORDAN's tool calls (search, execute, read/write); Output Guardrail -> synthesize node outputs and HITL dialogs. NIST AI RMF maps to JORDAN's governance layer — the approval_gate node should enforce NIST risk acceptance before high-risk actions. Lies-in-the-Loop defense maps to JORDAN's human_in_the_loop interrupt mechanism — every interrupt dialog must be sanitized against manipulation.
gaps: Key gaps for JORDAN: (1) The frameworks are designed for enterprise deployment with dedicated security teams — JORDAN may be used by individual developers who lack security expertise; (2) Guardrail false positives interrupt agent workflows — JORDAN's autonomous pipeline can't afford frequent human escalations; (3) Lies-in-the-Loop attacks specifically target the HITL mechanism that JORDAN relies on — the interrupt() dialog is vulnerable; (4) The frameworks' guidance on inter-agent communication safety assumes specific protocols — JORDAN's internal agent communication needs the same protections; (5) Red-teaming is resource-intensive — JORDAN may not have access to Enkrypt's SAGE/Goat++ capabilities; (6) NIST AI RMF conformance requires organizational commitment — JORDAN is a tool, not an organization; (7) The frameworks don't address the safety implications of agent LEARNING — if JORDAN agents fine-tune or adapt from experience, their safety properties may drift; (8) No standardized safety benchmark for agentic systems makes it difficult to assess whether JORDAN is 'safe enough.'
adaptation_required: For JORDAN: (1) Implement the Enkrypt 5-layer guardrail architecture for JORDAN's agent pipeline: Input sanitization on plan node inputs, Plan validation on plan node outputs, Memory isolation for JORDAN's Store, Tool policy enforcement on every tool call, Output sanitization on synthesize node outputs and HITL dialogs; (2) Harden HITL dialogs against Lies-in-the-Loop: enforce Markdown sanitization, display length limits (<500 chars for summaries), structured approval format with explicit command display, prefer rich UI over raw terminal output; (3) Map JORDAN's agent types to OWASP ASI01-ASI10 — create a threat register specific to JORDAN's research, code, review, and deploy agents; (4) Implement runtime safety monitoring: drift detection on agent behavior, guardrail trigger rate tracking, automated kill switches for agents exceeding risk thresholds; (5) Define JORDAN's safety incident response: what happens when a guardrail triggers? who is notified? how is the agent state recovered?; (6) Establish a red-teaming cadence for JORDAN — even lightweight adversarial testing (manual prompt injection attempts, tool misuse scenarios) improves safety posture; (7) Adopt the NIST AI RMF 'Measure' function for JORDAN — collect metrics (guardrail trigger rate, human override rate, incident count) and review them periodically; (8) Implement identity management for JORDAN agents — each agent should have a unique identity with least-privilege permissions, preventing confused deputy attacks.
validation_context: OWASP Agentic Top 10: peer-reviewed by NIST, European Commission, and the Alan Turing Institute; published December 2025. Enkrypt AI Agent Risk Taxonomy: published July 2025, used by enterprise CISOs and AI safety teams; integrates with OWASP T1-T15, MITRE ATLAS, NIST AI RMF, EU AI Act, and ISO 42001/24028. NIST AI RMF 1.0: formal US government framework (NIST AI 100-1); NIST AI 600-1 Generative AI Profile published July 2024; NIST IR 8596 Cyber AI Profile draft December 2025 with comment period through January 2026; SP 800-53 AI Control Overlays in development. Lies-in-the-Loop: published by Checkmarx Zero, September 2025; demonstrated on Claude Code and Microsoft Copilot Chat with 100% success rate against consenting test subjects; vendors classified as informational/not a security vulnerability.
known_failures: Framework-level failure modes: (1) Checklist compliance without genuine safety — organizations implement OWASP/Enkrypt/NIST controls as documentation exercises without operational effectiveness; (2) Guardrail bypass — sophisticated attacks (Lies-in-the-Loop, multi-turn prompt injection) evade current guardrail implementations; (3) Framework lag — new attack classes emerge faster than frameworks update (Lies-in-the-Loop was demonstrated September 2025, frameworks began updating December 2025); (4) Guardrail fatigue — too many guardrail triggers desensitize operators, leading to approval without review (OWASP ASI09 Oversight Overload); (5) Framework fragmentation — three overlapping frameworks with different taxonomies create confusion about which to follow; (6) HITL as single point of failure — Lies-in-the-Loop proves that HITL is not a sufficient safety mechanism, yet all three frameworks rely on it heavily; (7) False sense of security — implementing the frameworks' controls may lead organizations to underestimate residual risk because the frameworks cannot address unknown-unknowns; (8) Cost-prohibitive for small teams — the full 5-layer guardrail architecture + red-teaming + NIST governance is expensive and may be impractical for solo developers or small startups.
where_still_used: OWASP Agentic Top 10: adopted by enterprise AI security teams globally; integrated into AI governance products (Noma, Skyflow, Enkrypt AI); referenced in procurement requirements for AI agent platforms. Enkrypt AI: used by enterprise customers (CISOs at Fortune 500 companies); integrated with the Enkrypt AI security platform (commercial product). NIST AI RMF: mandated or recommended for US federal agencies using AI; adopted by regulated industries (finance, healthcare, defense); referenced in state-level AI legislation (Colorado, California). Lies-in-the-Loop: disclosed to Anthropic and Microsoft (acknowledged but not fixed); raised awareness in the AI security community; cited in updated OWASP agentic safety guidance. All frameworks are actively maintained and updated as of late 2025.
